frameworks.name: default-mode vs task-positive anticorrelation
frameworks.one_line: reciprocal large-scale brain systems framework probed under NMDA blockade

frameworks.name: NMDA receptor hypofunction model of schizophrenia
frameworks.one_line: glutamatergic dysfunction as mechanism for cognitive and psychotic symptoms

frameworks.name: biophysically realistic computational modeling of cortical disinhibition
frameworks.one_line: circuit-level disinhibition account proposed to explain large-scale connectivity disruption

frameworks.name: local circuit accounts of NMDA function
frameworks.one_line: prior work limited to local processes, extended here to large-scale systems

core_question: Does NMDA receptor antagonism disrupt the anticorrelation between default-mode and task-positive systems during working memory, and does this relate to cognition and schizophrenia-like symptoms?
key_assumptions: NMDA-mediated glutamatergic transmission underlies cortical computations for cognition
default-mode and task-positive systems are normally reciprocally anticorrelated
ketamine acts as a pharmacological model of NMDA hypofunction
manipulations: measurement of task-dependent activation and connectivity
key_findings: ketamine disrupted reciprocal activation between default-mode and task-positive systems
ketamine disrupted reciprocal connectivity between these systems
degree of disruption predicted working memory task performance
interpretations: NMDA hypofunction degrades large-scale anticorrelated system organization underlying cognition
cortical disinhibition offers a parsimonious circuit-level account of the disruption
